topic: customer scam attempt — asking for advice

turns.content: man I need some advice. had a customer come in today trying to pull something on me and I want to make sure I handle it right
Sorry to hear that. What happened exactly? The more details you can share, the better advice I can give you on how to handle it.

so this guy brought in a set of tires saying he bought them from us three weeks ago and they're defective. wants a full refund. problem is I dont recognize him and the tires he brought in are a brand I dont even carry

That's a pretty clear-cut scam attempt. The fact that you don't carry the brand is your strongest card here. A few things I'd recommend:

1. **Check your records** — pull your sales from the last month and confirm there's no matching transaction. If you use paper invoices or any kind of receipt system, that's your proof.
2. **Stay calm and professional** — don't accuse him of lying directly. Just say something like, "I've checked our records and we don't have a sale matching this, and we don't stock this brand."
3. **Don't give in** — some scammers count on shop owners wanting to avoid conflict. Stand firm.

Did he have any kind of receipt or proof of purchase from your shop?
nah no receipt. said he lost it. I told him I dont carry that brand and he started getting loud saying I was calling him a liar

Classic pressure tactic. Getting loud is designed to make you uncomfortable enough to just pay him to go away. Don't fall for it.

Here's what I'd do in the moment: stay even-toned and say something like, "Sir, I'm not calling you anything. I'm telling you what my records show and what brands I stock. Without a receipt and without a matching sale in my system, I can't process a refund."

If he escalates or gets threatening, you're well within your rights to ask him to leave. If he refuses, that becomes a trespassing issue and you can involve the police. Did he eventually leave?
yeah he left. cussed me out on the way to his car but he left. my concern is does he come back or try to leave bad reviews or something

Both are possible, so here's how to cover yourself:

**If he comes back:** Have a brief written record of today's incident — date, time, what he claimed, what brand the tires were, and that you don't carry them. If it becomes a pattern, that documentation helps if you need to involve law enforcement for harassment.

**If he leaves a bad review:** Don't panic. Respond professionally and factually. Something like, "We take all customer concerns seriously. Our records show no matching transaction, and we do not carry the brand in question. We invite anyone with a legitimate concern to contact us directly." That kind of response actually makes you look good to other people reading it.

Most scammers like this move on to the next target. He's probably not coming back.
thats good. I like that review response. should I be doing anything different going forward to protect myself from this kind of thing

A few simple habits that go a long way:

1. **Require a receipt for all returns/warranty claims.** Post a visible sign at the counter stating your return policy. If it's in writing and posted, it's much harder for someone to argue.
2. **Keep detailed sales records** — even if it's just a notebook, write down the customer name, tire brand/model, date, and amount for every sale. Takes 30 seconds.
3. **Take photos of tires before installation** if a customer seems difficult or if it's a high-dollar job. Quick phone picture, takes two seconds.
4. **Consider a no-cash-refund policy** — offer store credit only on returns. Scammers want cash. Store credit is useless to them.

Most of this is just building a paper trail. Makes scams nearly impossible to pull off.
I like that no cash refund idea. store credit only. thats smart

It's one of the best deterrents out there. Legitimate customers usually don't mind because they'll use the credit eventually anyway. But a scammer has zero use for store credit at a shop he never actually bought from.

Just make sure you post it clearly — at the counter, on your receipts if possible, and mention it verbally at the time of sale. That way nobody can say they didn't know.

Want help drafting the wording for a return policy sign you could print and put up?
nah I can figure that part out. I just wanted to make sure I wasnt missing something on how to deal with this kind of mess. appreciate the help brother

You handled it well — stayed calm, didn't cave, and the guy left. That's the main thing. Get that policy sign up when you get a chance and write down what happened today while it's fresh. After that, don't lose any sleep over it. These people usually don't come back twice.

Good luck, and hope the rest of the week is smoother for you.
